section.name: alias-antipatterns
section.summary: Common bash alias mistakes that break shells, cause subtle bugs, or reduce maintainability.

antipattern.description: Aliasing a command to itself with different flags, creating an infinite recursion loop.
antipattern.reason: alias rm="rm -rf" calls rm which calls rm again infinitely until the process limit is hit, making rm unusable. Use a different name or a function with \rm to bypass the alias.

antipattern.description: Missing quotes around alias values containing spaces or special characters.
antipattern.reason: alias ll=ls -la (unquoted) only aliases ll to ls; the -la is parsed as a separate token and ignored. The alias silently misbehaves rather than throwing an error.

antipattern.description: Putting complex logic (if/else, loops, subshells) directly in alias values.
antipattern.reason: Bash aliases do not support compound commands. They appear to work in simple cases but break in subtle ways with quoting, piping, and argument expansion. Use functions for any logic.

antipattern.description: Forgetting to source ~/.bash_aliases after making changes and wondering why new aliases do not work.
antipattern.reason: Aliases are loaded at shell startup. Editing the file does not update the running shell. Use `source ~/.bash_aliases` or a reload alias defined early in the file.
Mixing aliases into .bashrc makes the file harder to manage, harder to sync via dotfiles, and prevents isolated reload with a single source command.

example.code:
# Wrong: circular alias — infinite recursion alias rm="rm -rf"
# Wrong: unquoted, the flags are dropped silently alias ll=ls -la
# Wrong: complex logic in alias, breaks with pipes alias deploy="cd /srv/app && git pull && make install && systemctl restart app" # Correct version: define as a function deploy() { cd /srv/app && git pull && make install && systemctl restart app; }
# Wrong: aliases scattered in .bashrc between unrelated config export PATH="$HOME/bin:$PATH" alias gs="git status" # Don't put this here export NVM_DIR="$HOME/.nvm"
